quote: We've only been renting it for about a little over a year, but, we've owned the home for a long time.
stewart_read: John's phrasing is genuinely ambiguous. 'Renting it for about a little over a year' could mean (a) he is a landlord who recently moved back in, (b) he is a tenant who does not own the property, or (c) he is describing a prior rental period before purchasing. The rep responds 'Okay. Very nice.' and moves on without probing. Per dq_rules.not_homeowner, renters are a hard DQ — the rep needed one clarifying question here before proceeding.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have interpreted 'we've owned the home for a long time' as sufficient confirmation of ownership and moved on intentionally. Rejected — the ambiguity is real enough that a single follow-up question ('And you're the owner on the title?') was warranted before investing further in the call.
coaching_implication: Show Holland this line in the 1-on-1. Ask: 'What did you hear here?' If Holland heard ownership confirmed, walk through why the renting language creates ambiguity. The probe is one sentence: 'And you're the owner on the title?' Skipping it on a hard-DQ item is a qualification gap regardless of how the call ends.

quote: How long have you been living in this home for?
stewart_read: This is the second and last verify item the rep completes. The call ends with zero data collected on interest_in_solar, utility_company, average_bill, roof_type_age, prior_designs, or title/co-owners. The rep abandoned the verify section after two items to conduct a Google Maps site assessment. A complete verify failure means the rep has no qualification data to support a callback, no bill anchor, and no emotional hook — even if the design team clears the shading question, there is nothing to work with.
coaching_implication: Use this call to walk Holland through the verify sequence item by item. The question is not 'why did you look at Google Maps' — it is 'what were you supposed to be doing at 00:47 and what did you do instead?' The verify section is the rep's job; the site assessment is the design team's job.

quote: So I see that you have some trees around you. Are all of those trees still there?
stewart_read: This is the origin point of the call's failure. The rep is on Google Maps mid-call and pivots the conversation to a satellite-image shading assessment before completing the verify section. This is setter scope creep in its clearest form: the rep is doing design-team work — site evaluation — rather than qualification work. The setter's job is to gather information and schedule the closer; shading analysis from a satellite image is not in that scope.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been checking the address to confirm it was a real residential property, and the tree question was incidental. Rejected — the rep's subsequent DQ language ('I don't think that the panels will be able to get enough sun') confirms this was a deliberate site assessment, not an address check.
coaching_implication: This is the coaching entry point for the whole call. Ask Holland: 'Whose job is it to evaluate shading from a satellite image?' The answer is the design team. The setter's job is to get John on the calendar so the design team can do that evaluation. Once Holland names the scope boundary, the rest of the call's failures follow logically.

quote: So I think that with those trees still there, I don't think that the panels will be able to get enough sun, for them to be super fully fully beneficial to you.
stewart_read: The rep issues a unilateral shading disqualification based on a Google Maps satellite image, without design-team input, without completing verify, and before John has raised any concern. This is not a DQ the setter is authorized to make — shading analysis requires the design team. The rep's own language at 02:25 ('I can reach out to my design team') confirms the design team is the correct authority, which makes this DQ retroactively self-contradicting.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: the rep genuinely believed the shading was disqualifying and was trying to save John's time. Strategic read: the rep was setting up a 'design team review' callback as a softer close. The reactive read is more supported — the rep's language is apologetic and exit-oriented, not setup-oriented, and no callback time was locked.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 01:09 moment. The DQ at 01:24 is the consequence of the scope creep at 01:09. The coaching move is not 'don't DQ people' — it is 'you are not the design team.' The correct path when Google Maps shows trees: note it, complete verify, and let the design team assess at the appointment.

quote: Once I once I have that side gets a lot. But the trees really only only give shade on the portion of the house and only a part of the day, if that makes a difference.
stewart_read: John is not accepting the DQ — he is advocating for his own home's solar viability. He describes partial shading on one portion of the roof for part of the day, which is substantively different from full-canopy coverage. This is an enthusiasm signal: a customer who pushes back on a disqualification with specific site knowledge is a motivated buyer. The rep does not engage this information at all.
coaching_implication: Show Holland this line and ask: 'What is John telling you here?' John is saying his roof has viable sun exposure. The correct response is not a second disqualifier — it is 'That's actually really helpful to know. Let me get you in front of our design team so they can look at the specific angles.' This is the moment the call could have been saved.

quote: It's a ton of space to put panels up there, unfortunately.
stewart_read: Fourteen seconds elapsed between John's last word (02:06) and this response — consistent with the rep continuing to review the satellite image rather than processing John's rebuttal. The rep introduces a second independent disqualifier (roof space) that does not track John's shading argument. This is not multi-angle objection handling; it is the rep conducting a parallel site assessment and generating a new exit reason. The two disqualifiers are additive scope creep, not a response to John.
alternative_hypothesis: Knowledge-gap read: the rep heard John's shading rebuttal, could not counter it, and pivoted to a different concern. Rejected — the 14-second gap and the non-sequitur nature of the roof-space comment (John said nothing about roof size) point to continued satellite-image review rather than a response to John's words.
coaching_implication: Use the 14-second gap as a coaching anchor. Ask Holland: 'What were you doing during those 14 seconds?' If the answer is 'looking at the map,' that confirms the rep was doing design-team work in real time rather than listening to the customer. The coaching point: when a customer pushes back, the rep's job is to listen and route — not to find a second reason to exit.

quote: I can reach out to my design team and see if they would be able to do anything. And then if they can, I'll give you a callback, and we could totally get you scheduled. Okay?
stewart_read: The rep's own language here reveals the correct path — the design team is the authority on shading and roof space. But the offer is a conditional callback with no locked time, no verify data to support it, and no commitment from John beyond a passive 'Okay.' Per the schema, callbacks with locked times are materially different from 'we'll touch base' outcomes on no-show rate. This is the latter.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep is using the callback as a soft close to keep the lead alive after a failed DQ attempt. Rejected — no time was locked, no verify data was collected, and the rep has nothing to hand off to the design team. The callback is an exit with a courtesy label.
coaching_implication: If the design team review is the right next step, the rep should have said: 'Let me get you on the calendar for Thursday — our design team will pull the actual shading analysis for your address and walk you through what's possible. Does morning or afternoon work better?' A locked time converts this from a lost lead to a recoverable one.
